event: TDD self-tests written first (chains, leakage, scoring) — watched fail, then implemented modules. 25 tests green.
Built dataset from CSV: 393,970 rows → 287,605 chains.
Caught data bug: edit_timestamp is a datetime STRING; numeric coercion nulled it and reversed chain order. Fixed to datetime parse.
After fix: links_ok 99.97% (spec 99.997%), task_then_user 73,600 (spec ~73,643). Chain reconstruction validated.
Glossary had 0 per-city terms (global subsumed them). Redefined: global = spans >=2 cities; per-city = recurs within one city. Now 5,894 global + 3,543 per-city across 11 cities.
Verified claude -p (sonnet, tools off, system-prompt override): 3/3 clean numbered lines, ~4.8s/call. Real homophone fix ηλίκιο→λύκειο. No API key — OAuth.
Built heuristic text-only categorizer (no audio/NER): person/place/org merged to named_entity; verb/noun/article to morph_grammar. 8 sanity tests.
Stratified sample: 1000 rows, 100/category over 10 categories. A/B run launched in tmux (workers=8, checkpointed).
Tightened glossary retrieval (length>=4 guard, cutoff 88/90) to cut short-token distractor noise.
First A/B run: 259/1000 ok, 741 exit-1 failures after ~17min — sustained-concurrency rate/usage throttle (empty stderr).
Fixed resume to exclude error rows; longer backoff (5/15/30/60/90s). Relaunched at workers=4, resuming 741 failed rows.
Enqueued Codex (high effort) review of scoring + categorizer logic.

Codex review acted on: scoring rewritten (multiset-based) — pure deletions now need actual removal; hallucinated insertions count as harm; duplicate-token robustness. Categorizer: number_date catches digit changes/removals, named_entity before insertion_deletion, final_sigma reachable. +4 scoring tests, 28 total green.
Re-run hit the Claude session limit ('resets 6am Europe/Athens'); it stalled overnight retrying, then completed after the 6am reset. Final: 956/1000 rows scored, 44 still missing.

RESULT (956 rows, strict scoring): glossary HELPS named_entity +9.9pp (the hypothesized win) but mildly HURTS most other categories (acronym -6.5, number_date -6.0, accent -5.9) via retrieval-noise overcorrection. task_then_user residuals are hardest (42.6% baseline). Conclusion: apply glossary selectively to entities, not globally.
Quota analysis: 162/956 rows (17%) had an empty glossary, so their 2nd call duplicated baseline — pure waste. Planning selective-arm + cheaper-backend strategy to finish smartly.
Empty-glossary skip implemented: when retrieval returns no terms the arms are identical, so the 2nd call is reused — saves ~17% of calls on every run. Resuming the 44 missing rows.

Pluggable backends added (eval/backends.py): claude (sonnet/haiku via OAuth gate), codex (gpt-5.4-mini low via codex-bridge, separate quota), gemini (stub — needs CLI/key). Codex backend validated end-to-end. run_ab + segment take --backend/--model and record provenance per row.
Per-utterance A/B complete: 1000/1000 rows scored on sonnet.

Segment proxy pass complete (100/100, sonnet). Gap: context doesn't help these categories on the proxy (partly a line-count parse confound), but the glossary named_entity win HOLDS in segments too (+11pp: 36.8%→47.9%). Per-utterance sweep is a sound proxy.
Harness committed + pushed to origin/eval/fix-task-harness (code + docs + logs; data/ gitignored by project convention).

Autoresearch improvement loop built (eval/improve_loop.py): editable artifact = system prompt, LLM-researcher proposes mutations. Seeds: baseline, +glossary, +anti-overcorrection, +acronym rule, combo.

Codex (high effort) reviewed the loop design. Acted on it: PRIMARY metric switched to Human Intervention Rate (HIR = fraction of utterances still needing a human edit); WER added as secondary error-volume metric (4 new tests). Selection now minimises HIR; net kept only as a search surrogate.

Validation hardened per Codex: same backend for selection AND validation (avoids measuring transferability instead of improvement); test set enlarged to 140 rows and used ONCE; paired stats added — #improved/#regressed, paired bootstrap 95% CI on HIR diff, exact McNemar p; predeclared stopping rule (max rounds + min HIR gain, stop after 2 stalls).

haiku as eval backend gave garbage signal: HIR 83-93%, overcorrection 1-3.9x (haiku over-generates, ignores 'no explanations'). Loop was selecting for 'least runaway output', not quality. Killed; archived to leaderboard.haiku.jsonl.

Quota pivot: Claude usage hit 77% mid-run. Switched selection to codex gpt-5.5-low (separate quota, frontier-quality so it follows the constrained numbered-line format, unlike haiku). Reserved remaining Claude/sonnet quota for production-transfer validation.

Codex gpt-5.5 selection loop succeeded on DEV (no stall): HIR 0.643 (baseline) -> 0.476 (r20, LLM-proposed, anti-overcorrection lineage), -16.7pp / -26% rel. Winner uses NO glossary. r20 prompt adds a 'reconstruct what was spoken, not stylistically better' principle + grounded Greek per-category examples.

HELD-OUT TEST (n=140, codex gpt-5.5, used once) REFUTES the dev gain: HIR 0.500 (baseline) -> 0.521 (r20), +2.1pp WORSE; WER flat; improved 6 / regressed 9; McNemar p=0.61; 95% CI [-0.079,+0.029] contains 0. The loop OVERFIT to the 42 dev failures the proposer saw. Honest conclusion: text-only prompt tuning yields NO generalizable HIR reduction on these hardest task_then_user residuals -> they are not cheaply prompt-fixable; routing points them to ASR finetune / audio-grounded correction.

Sonnet transfer check (r20 vs baseline, same held-out test n=140): HIR 0.679 -> 0.664 (+1.4pp better but NOT significant; McNemar p=0.77; 95% CI [-0.029,+0.064] contains 0; improved 7 / regressed 5). WER did drop -0.070 (0.251->0.181). Both backends agree: no significant HIR transfer (dev gain was overfit), though r20 shrinks residual-error magnitude (WER) and is not harmful on sonnet. Note: gpt-5.5 baseline (HIR 0.50) is a stronger corrector on these residuals than sonnet baseline (HIR 0.68).

Built anti-overfit 'frozen-pool' harness (run-pool) per Codex review of the overfit: stratified deterministic propose/select split (strat_split, +3 TDD tests, 38 total green); proposer blind to the select half; candidates generated ONCE then ranked once on select (no iterative holdout reuse, Dwork et al.); diversity foci; winner's-curse guards (logged budget, deterministic tie-break to simpler/incumbent prompt); bigger data (dev=343 propose203/select140, fresh held-out test=245). Launched on codex gpt-5.5 (free quota) in parallel with the sonnet transfer check.

Built deterministic fuzzy name/toponym corrector (eval/fuzzy_correct.py, +8 TDD tests, 46 total green) + standalone eval (eval/fuzzy_eval.py), per Codex design review: separate match-vs-scoring normalisation, length-dependent edit limits, candidate margin, name-like typing proxy, phrase-first n-gram matching. Tests the hypothesis: fix names in CODE (outside the prompt) to keep the named_entity win without cross-category overcorrection.

Deterministic fuzzy-only REFUTED on held-out test (245). Across all operating points (strict max-dist-1/min-len-6/margin-2 -> unfiltered): precision stays ~12-15%, named_entity is net-neutral-to-negative even on its OWN category (strict +4/-4, v2 +11/-17), collateral 12-46 worsened per 100 non-entity rows. Capitalization typing did NOT help (v2==v3) because the harm comes from capitalized NAMES pulling valid common words (e.g. Βάσω<-βάση) — Codex's predicted #1 failure mode.

Clean-control set built (gold_final_retention, Wilson 95% CI, 1500 unique human-approved finals) and immediately proved its worth: deterministic fuzzy CORRUPTS already-clean text badly — strict retention 0.859 (14% corrupted), v2 retention 0.429 (57% corrupted). Non-deployable. Conclusion: blind fuzzy matching lacks the context to tell a misspelled name from a valid word; the LLM glossary-in-prompt beat it on entities (+9.9pp) precisely because it uses context. Right architecture = GATED: LLM/NER identifies a name span, THEN snap to canonical via glossary.

Local NER gate evaluated: GLiNER (urchade/gliner_multi-v2.1, multilingual, Apache-2.0, runs in Python now and in Node via GLiNER.js/ONNX). On Greek it tags MISSPELLED names from context ('Κύριε Σουμπάκη'->person 0.83, 'Κολυμβητική Ομοσπονδία'->org 0.95). Added allowed_spans NER gate to fuzzy_correct (+1 TDD test, 47 total green) and eval/ner_gate_eval.py. Rejected elNER (the user's link): it is a CORPUS, CC BY-NC-SA non-commercial, unmaintained -> cannot ship in OpenCouncil. spaCy el_core_news ~51% F1 too weak; fine-tuned GreekBERT ~85-91% F1 the higher-accuracy alternative (deploy via Python sidecar on the droplet).

NER gating helps a lot but is still NOT deployable with the GLOBAL glossary. Held-out test 245 + 600 clean finals: gating cut collateral 4x and lifted clean retention 0.452->0.832 (permissive) and precision 0.115->0.239; strict gated reached retention 0.957 but named_entity recall collapsed to ~0. No operating point gives BOTH usable recall AND ~99% clean retention: within the dense 5894-name global glossary, fuzzy still snaps already-correct names to near neighbours. KEY INSIGHT: the wrong candidate set is the problem — fuzzy should match against the SMALL per-meeting roster (partiesWithPeople, ~tens of names) that OpenCouncil already has, not the global mined glossary. NER-gate + per-meeting roster is the architecture to test next (needs roster ingestion). For now the LLM (roster-in-prompt) remains the best name corrector because it knows when a name is already correct.

Gate comparison (GreekBERT vs GLiNER) + per-meeting candidate set. (2) GreekBERT-NER (amichailidis/bert-base-greek-uncased-v1-finetuned-ner) beats GLiNER as a gate on every metric (precision 0.339 vs 0.239, recall 0.257 vs 0.20, retention 0.855 vs 0.832) because it does NOT false-positive common words (e.g. 'θάλασσα'). But conclusion unchanged: the gate is not the bottleneck. (1) Shrinking the candidate set is what helps: GreekBERT-gate + leakage-safe per-meeting roster proxy (recurring names, ~116) -> precision 0.390, retention 0.908, vs global 5894 -> 0.855. Monotone trend (5894/0.855 -> 381/0.868 -> 116/0.908) confirms: smaller/cleaner candidate set raises both precision and clean retention. Still ~9% corruption at ~116 (proxy is still too big/noisy). Decisive test needs the REAL ~30-name roster (partiesWithPeople) ingested; trend predicts it lands materially higher. Best deterministic config so far: GreekBERT-gate + per-meeting roster, but deployment still requires ~99% clean retention on real no-edit traffic.
